content.text: Design Areas

The Cloud Adoption Framework structures all landing zone decisions into eight design areas. Each design area is a domain of decisions that must be made before workloads can be deployed safely and consistently. The first four are critical — they constrain the architecture of everything else and are hardest to change after initial deployment. The remaining four are operational and can be refined iteratively after launch.

attrs.alt: Eight CAF design areas — critical decisions (left) and operational decisions (right)

content.text: Design area decisions compound. A billing model choice (EA vs. MCA) determines what automation is available for subscription vending. An identity model choice determines how RBAC is enforced. Network topology determines where Private DNS zones live. Sequence these decisions before writing any Bicep.
Design Area 1: Billing and Microsoft Entra ID Tenants

The billing design area covers the agreement type (Enterprise Agreement, Microsoft Customer Agreement, or Cloud Solution Provider) and whether the organization uses a single Entra ID tenant or multiple tenants. EA customers use enrollment accounts to create subscriptions programmatically; MCA customers use billing profiles. Multi-tenant architectures are unusual and add significant complexity to identity federation and cross-tenant resource management — the default is always single-tenant.
Decision
Options
Key consequence
Agreement type
EA, MCA, CSP, Pay-as-you-go
EA/MCA support programmatic subscription creation via ARM APIs; PAYG requires manual subscription creation in the portal
Tenant count
Single vs. multi-tenant
Multi-tenant requires B2B guest user management for cross-tenant access; single tenant is always preferred
Subscription vending
Manual vs. automated (via IaC + ARM API)
Automated vending enables self-service landing zones for app teams; manual vending creates platform team bottlenecks
EA enrollment hierarchy
Department > Account > Subscription vs. flat
Department grouping enables cost reporting by organizational unit independent of management groups
Design Area 2: Identity and Access Management

The identity design area covers Entra ID tenant configuration, on-premises Active Directory synchronization, RBAC model design, and Privileged Identity Management. Key decisions are: whether to use Entra ID Connect to sync on-premises AD identities (required for hybrid workloads), whether to use Privileged Identity Management for just-in-time admin access (strongly recommended), and which RBAC roles to assign at which scope (management group, subscription, or resource group).
Decision
Recommended default
Why
AD sync
Entra ID Connect (sync mode) if on-premises AD exists
Hybrid workloads (VM domain join, SQL auth) require synced identities
Admin access model
Privileged Identity Management (PIM) with JIT activation
Eliminates permanent standing privileges; provides audit log for all admin actions
RBAC scope
Assign roles at resource group scope where possible; subscription scope for platform access
Principle of least privilege; resource group scope limits blast radius
Break-glass accounts
2 cloud-only emergency accounts in tenant root, no PIM, MFA-exempted
Required for tenant lockout recovery when PIM or Conditional Access fails
Design Area 3: Resource Organization

Resource organization covers the management group hierarchy, subscription topology, naming conventions, and tagging strategy. This is the design area most directly covered by the rest of this subject area. The key architectural decision is how many management groups to create (avoid deep hierarchies — CAF recommends no more than three levels below the tenant root) and whether to use subscription-per-workload or shared subscriptions.
Design Area 4: Network Topology and Connectivity

Network topology is the most technically constrained design area because address space decisions cannot be changed after VNet creation without recreating subnets and all resources within them. Key decisions: hub-spoke vs. Azure Virtual WAN, VNet address allocation strategy, ExpressRoute vs. VPN vs. Internet-only connectivity, and centralized vs. distributed DNS. Most enterprise environments choose hub-spoke with Azure Firewall for inspection and Private DNS zones in the hub for Azure service name resolution.

# Design Area 4 — Network Topology: hub VNet address planning
# The hub VNet address space must not overlap with any spoke or on-premises range.
# Typical hub range: 10.0.0.0/16 with well-known subnet assignments.

# Hub subnet layout (RFC 1918 10.0.0.0/16):
#   10.0.0.0/26   AzureFirewallSubnet         (Azure Firewall — fixed name)
#   10.0.0.64/26  AzureFirewallManagementSubnet (forced tunnelling — fixed name)
#   10.0.1.0/27   GatewaySubnet               (VPN/ExpressRoute — fixed name)
#   10.0.2.0/27   AzureBastionSubnet           (Bastion — fixed name)
#   10.0.3.0/24   snet-hub-mgmt-prod-eastus   (Management jump hosts)

# Spoke address allocation strategy (per application landing zone):
#   /22 per spoke (1022 usable IPs) — supports AKS node pools
#   Allocate sequentially from 10.1.0.0/8 starting at 10.1.0.0/22, 10.1.4.0/22, ...

az network vnet create \
  --name vnet-hub-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-connectivity-prod-eastus \
  --address-prefixes 10.0.0.0/16

# Create required subnets with exact names Azure demands
az network vnet subnet create --name AzureFirewallSubnet \
  --vnet-name vnet-hub-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-connectivity-prod-eastus \
  --address-prefix 10.0.0.0/26

az network vnet subnet create --name GatewaySubnet \
  --vnet-name vnet-hub-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-connectivity-prod-eastus \
  --address-prefix 10.0.1.0/27

az network vnet subnet create --name AzureBastionSubnet \
  --vnet-name vnet-hub-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-connectivity-prod-eastus \
  --address-prefix 10.0.2.0/27

# Spoke VNet (application landing zone — payments workload)
az network vnet create \
  --name vnet-payments-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-payments-network-prod-eastus \
  --address-prefixes 10.1.0.0/22

az network vnet subnet create --name snet-payments-app-prod-eastus \
  --vnet-name vnet-payments-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-payments-network-prod-eastus \
  --address-prefix 10.1.0.0/24

az network vnet subnet create --name snet-payments-data-prod-eastus \
  --vnet-name vnet-payments-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-payments-network-prod-eastus \
  --address-prefix 10.1.1.0/24
Design Area 5: Security

The security design area covers Microsoft Defender for Cloud (secure score, recommendations, threat protection), Zero Trust principles (explicit verification, least privilege, assume breach), encryption at rest and in transit, secret management (Key Vault per application or centralized), and security monitoring (Sentinel, Defender XDR). Defender for Cloud's CSPM tier is enabled at the management group level via a single Policy assignment; workload protection plans are enabled per subscription.
Design Area 6: Management

The management design area defines the operations baseline: a central Log Analytics workspace for all diagnostic logs and platform telemetry, Azure Update Manager for patch compliance, Azure Backup policies for data protection, and automation runbooks for routine remediation. The central Log Analytics workspace is provisioned in the management platform subscription and connected to all other subscriptions via Policy-driven diagnostic settings.

# Design Area 6 — Management: central Log Analytics workspace
# One workspace per environment tier (prod / non-prod) collects all diagnostic logs.

MGMT_SUB='<management-sub-id>'
az account set --subscription $MGMT_SUB

# Create the management resource group and Log Analytics workspace
az group create \
  --name rg-management-prod-eastus \
  --location eastus \
  --tags Environment=prod Application=management

az monitor log-analytics workspace create \
  --name log-management-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-management-prod-eastus \
  --location eastus \
  --sku PerGB2018 \
  --retention-time 90

LOG_WS_ID=$(az monitor log-analytics workspace show \
  --name log-management-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-management-prod-eastus \
  --query id -o tsv)

# Route Azure Activity Log from all subscriptions to the central workspace
# (Run once per subscription — normally done via Policy DINE effect)
for SUB_ID in $(az account list --query '[].id' -o tsv); do
  az monitor diagnostic-settings create \
    --name 'send-activity-log-to-central' \
    --resource "/subscriptions/$SUB_ID" \
    --workspace $LOG_WS_ID \
    --logs '[{"category":"Administrative","enabled":true},{"category":"Security","enabled":true},{"category":"Alert","enabled":true},{"category":"Policy","enabled":true}]'
  echo "Configured activity log routing for $SUB_ID"
done

# Enable Update Manager for all VMs in the management group
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'enable-update-manager' \
  --display-name 'Enable periodic assessment for VMs' \
  --policy 'bd876905-5b84-4f73-ab2d-2e7a7c4568d9' \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-workloads' \
  --identity-type SystemAssigned \
  --location eastus
Design Area 7: Governance

The governance design area translates organizational policies into Azure Policy definitions and assignments. The typical approach is a baseline Policy initiative assigned at the tenant root management group (covering all subscriptions) plus workload-tier initiatives assigned at the mg-workloads and mg-platform management groups. This produces a layered policy stack without requiring per-subscription assignments.
Design Area 8: Platform Automation and DevOps

The platform automation design area defines how the landing zone itself is managed as code. This includes the choice of IaC tool (Bicep for Azure-native, Terraform for multi-cloud), the CI/CD pipeline for platform changes, the subscription vending process (how new application landing zones are provisioned on request), and the GitOps model for policy-as-code. The ALZ Bicep and Terraform accelerators from Microsoft provide reference implementations that can be forked and customized.
Component
Bicep approach
Terraform approach
Management group hierarchy
az deployment tenant create with MG ARM resources
azurerm_management_group resource
Policy definitions/assignments
Bicep policy modules at MG scope
azurerm_policy_definition + azurerm_policy_assignment
Platform subscriptions
az deployment tenant or MCA API + Bicep sub-scope
azurerm_subscription (EA/MCA only)
Application LZ vending
GitHub Actions workflow calling Bicep per-subscription
Terraform workspace per subscription in a loop
Hub VNet + Firewall
Bicep module: hub-connectivity.bicep
azurerm_virtual_network + azurerm_firewall
Design Area 2 Deep Dive: Identity and RBAC Model

The identity design area produces three concrete outputs: a synchronization decision (Entra ID Connect for hybrid, cloud-only for greenfield), a privileged access model (Privileged Identity Management with JIT elevation for all admin roles), and an RBAC model that defines which roles exist, at which scope they are assigned, and which groups hold them. The custom Landing Zone Owner role below reduces the blast radius of application team access by blocking policy modification while granting full resource control.

# Design Area 2 — Identity: PIM activation and RBAC model setup

# 1. Activate Privileged Identity Management for a management group
# (PIM is enabled tenant-wide automatically in Entra ID P2 tenants)

# 2. Create a custom RBAC role for application landing zone owners
# This role allows full control within a subscription but cannot
# modify policy assignments or management group settings.
cat > app-lz-owner-role.json << 'ENDJSON'
{
  "Name": "Application Landing Zone Owner",
  "Description": "Full access within a subscription; cannot modify policy or management groups",
  "Actions": ["*"],
  "NotActions": [
    "Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/write",
    "Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/delete",
    "Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/write",
    "Microsoft.Authorization/policySetDefinitions/write",
    "Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/write",
    "Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/delete"
  ],
  "DataActions": [],
  "NotDataActions": [],
  "AssignableScopes": ["/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-workloads"]
}
ENDJSON

az role definition create --role-definition @app-lz-owner-role.json

# 3. Create a PIM-eligible assignment for the payments team as Landing Zone Owner
# (Requires Entra ID P2 and the PIM extension — done via Azure Portal or Graph API)
# CLI equivalent (requires az rest with PIM API):
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --resource 'https://management.azure.com' --query accessToken -o tsv)
SUB_ID='<payments-sub-id>'
ROLE_ID=$(az role definition list --name 'Application Landing Zone Owner' --query '[0].name' -o tsv)
TEAM_OID='<payments-team-aad-group-object-id>'

# Create eligible assignment via PIM API
curl -s -X PUT \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleEligibilityScheduleRequests/$(uuidgen)?api-version=2022-04-01-preview" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d "{
    \"properties\": {
      \"roleDefinitionId\": \"/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions/$ROLE_ID\",
      \"principalId\": \"$TEAM_OID\",
      \"requestType\": \"AdminAssign\",
      \"scheduleInfo\": {
        \"startDateTime\": null,
        \"expiration\": {\"type\": \"NoExpiration\"}
      }
    }
  }" | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '\"(name|status)\"'
Design Area 8 Deep Dive: Subscription Vending

Subscription vending is the automated process that converts a landing zone request (workload name, environment, team email) into a provisioned subscription with the spoke VNet, RBAC, and baseline Policy applied. In EA tenants, subscriptions are created via the REST API (az rest to the createSubscription endpoint); in MCA tenants, via the Billing Profiles API. A GitHub Actions workflow is the common execution engine — it receives the request as workflow_dispatch inputs and drives the full provisioning sequence.

# Design Area 8 — Automation: subscription vending via GitHub Actions
# When an application team requests a new landing zone, this workflow
# creates the subscription (EA/MCA API), moves it to the workloads MG,
# and deploys the spoke VNet via the app-lz Bicep module.

# .github/workflows/vend-landing-zone.yml (abbreviated)
cat << 'EOF'
name: Vend Application Landing Zone
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      workload:
        description: 'Workload name (lowercase, alphanumeric)'
        required: true
      environment:
        description: 'Environment (dev/test/prod)'
        required: true
        default: 'dev'
      team_email:
        description: 'Team email for RBAC assignment'
        required: true

jobs:
  vend:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Azure Login
        uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          creds: ${{ secrets.AZURE_PLATFORM_CREDENTIALS }}

      - name: Create subscription (EA API)
        run: |
          # EA enrollment account subscription creation
          az rest --method POST \
            --uri 'https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Billing/enrollmentAccounts/<ea-account>/providers/Microsoft.Subscription/createSubscription?api-version=2019-10-01-preview' \
            --body "{\"displayName\":\"sub-${{ inputs.workload }}-${{ inputs.environment }}\",\"offerType\":\"MS-AZR-0017P\"}"

      - name: Move subscription to workloads MG
        run: |
          az account management-group subscription add \
            --name mg-workloads \
            --subscription "sub-${{ inputs.workload }}-${{ inputs.environment }}"

      - name: Deploy spoke VNet and RBAC
        run: |
          az deployment sub create \
            --template-file bicep/app-landing-zone.bicep \
            --location eastus \
            --subscription "sub-${{ inputs.workload }}-${{ inputs.environment }}" \
            --parameters workload=${{ inputs.workload }} environment=${{ inputs.environment }} teamEmail=${{ inputs.team_email }}
EOF
Validating Design Area Implementation

Before accepting the first application landing zone request, run a validation checklist that confirms each design area is implemented: management group hierarchy exists, baseline Policy initiative is assigned, Log Analytics workspace is live, hub VNet has the required named subnets, Azure Firewall is running, and Private DNS zones are linked to the hub VNet. These checks can be scripted and added as a gate in the platform CI/CD pipeline.

# Validate that all design area decisions are captured before provisioning
# This checklist is run as a policy gate in the landing zone request process.

# Check 1: Management group hierarchy exists
echo '--- MG Hierarchy ---'
az account management-group list \
  --query '[].{Name:name, DisplayName:displayName}' -o table

# Check 2: Baseline Policy initiative is assigned at the root MG
echo '--- Policy Assignments at Root MG ---'
az policy assignment list \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp' \
  --query '[].{Name:name, DisplayName:displayName, Scope:scope}' -o table

# Check 3: Central Log Analytics workspace exists in the management subscription
echo '--- Central Log Analytics Workspace ---'
az monitor log-analytics workspace list \
  --resource-group rg-management-prod-eastus \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Sku:sku.name, RetentionDays:retentionInDays}' -o table

# Check 4: Hub VNet with required subnets exists in connectivity subscription
echo '--- Hub VNet Subnets ---'
az network vnet subnet list \
  --vnet-name vnet-hub-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-connectivity-prod-eastus \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Prefix:addressPrefix}' -o table

# Check 5: Azure Firewall is running
echo '--- Azure Firewall ---'
az network firewall show \
  --name afw-hub-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-connectivity-prod-eastus \
  --query '{Name:name, State:provisioningState}' -o table

# Check 6: Private DNS zones exist for common services
echo '--- Private DNS Zones ---'
az network private-dns zone list \
  --resource-group rg-connectivity-prod-eastus \
  --query '[].{Zone:name}' -o table

echo 'Design area validation complete.'
Auditing Design Area Coverage with Resource Graph

Resource Graph can detect subscriptions that have drifted from the landing zone architecture: subscriptions not under the governance management group, subscriptions without a spoke VNet, subscriptions where Defender for Cloud plans are still on the Free tier, and subscriptions not routing activity logs to the central workspace. Run these queries as part of a weekly governance report.

// Resource Graph: verify design area implementation across all subscriptions

// Check: subscriptions outside any management group (orphaned from governance)
ResourceContainers
| where type =~ 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions'
| where managementGroupAncestorsChain !has 'mg-corp'
| project name, subscriptionId

// Check: subscriptions without a VNet (likely not using spoke pattern)
ResourceContainers
| where type =~ 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions'
| join kind=leftouter (
    Resources
    | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworks'
    | where name startswith 'vnet-'
    | summarize VNetCount = count() by subscriptionId
) on subscriptionId
| where isnull(VNetCount) or VNetCount == 0
| project name, subscriptionId

// Check: subscriptions without Defender for Cloud plans enabled
SecurityResources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.security/pricings'
| where tostring(properties.pricingTier) =~ 'Free'
| summarize FreePlans = count(), Plans = make_list(name) by subscriptionId
| where FreePlans > 0
| order by FreePlans desc

// Check: subscriptions without diagnostic settings sending to central workspace
// (Indirect check: look for the diagnostic settings resource)
Resources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.insights/diagnosticsettings'
| where tostring(properties.workspaceId) contains 'log-management-prod'
| summarize HasCentralDiag = count() by subscriptionId
| join kind=rightouter (
    ResourceContainers | where type =~ 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions'
    | project subscriptionId
) on subscriptionId
| where isnull(HasCentralDiag)
| project subscriptionId
Common Design Area Mistakes
Design area
Common mistake
Consequence
Fix
Billing/Tenant
Using Pay-as-you-go subscriptions for production workloads
No programmatic subscription creation; manual portal steps for each LZ
Move to EA or MCA before scaling to >5 subscriptions
Identity
Assigning Owner directly (not via PIM) to application teams at subscription scope
Permanent standing admin access; no audit trail for privilege use
Create eligible PIM assignments; require justification and approval for activation
Resource organization
Mixing prod and dev workloads in the same subscription
Policy exceptions for dev can create prod security gaps
Separate subscriptions per environment; enforce via management group structure
Networking
Allocating overlapping address spaces across spokes
VNet peering fails; on-premises route conflicts
Maintain a centralized IPAM document or tool before allocating any VNet range
Security
Enabling only the Free tier of Defender for Cloud
No threat detection, no security recommendations, no secure score
Enable Defender CSPM at minimum on all production subscriptions via Policy
Management
Separate Log Analytics workspaces per subscription
No cross-subscription correlation; multiple workspaces multiplies cost
One workspace per environment tier (prod, non-prod) in the management subscription
Governance
Assigning policies per subscription instead of at the MG level
New subscriptions are ungoverned until manually assigned
All baseline policy assignments go on the MG; per-subscription is the exception, not the rule
Automation
Manually provisioning landing zones via the portal
No audit trail; inconsistent configuration; platform team bottleneck
Automate all LZ provisioning via IaC and CI/CD before accepting the first workload request
Summary

The eight CAF landing zone design areas provide a structured framework for making the architectural decisions that constrain everything else in an Azure deployment. The four critical areas — billing/tenant, identity, resource organization, and networking — must be decided first because they are hard to change after workloads exist. The four operational areas — security, management, governance, and automation — can be refined iteratively. Resolving each design area before provisioning resources prevents the architectural debt that accumulates when shortcuts are taken during early cloud adoption.
